Tokyo · Kyoto · Fuji · Hokkaido · Kumano Kodo · Ise · Hiroshima


A journey through temple and forest, bowl and bar. From Zen gardens to pilgrimage mountains — designed for a family of curious eaters, serious thinkers, and kids who might actually love it.

Route Map

Japan — All Destinations

Numbered in order of visit. Click each pin for details. The dashed line traces the approximate route.

① Tokyo ② Kyoto ③ Fuji/Hakone ④ Hokkaido ⑤ Kumano ⑥ Ise ⑦ Hiroshima
Pre-Departure

Before You Go

Several experiences require advance booking — some months ahead. These are non-negotiable if you want the full trip.

✅ International Flights — Booked
Confirmation: JCYY32 (United Airlines) · 5 passengers
Jul 8  UA611   Tampa (TPA) → San Francisco (SFO)  6:42 AM → 9:19 AM
Jul 8  UA35    San Francisco (SFO) → Osaka (KIX)     11:10 AM → Jul 9, 2:50 PM
Jul 9  NH992  Osaka (KIX) → Tokyo Haneda (HND)   3:50 PM → 5:20 PM  ⚠ 1hr connection
Jul 29 UA838  Tokyo Narita (NRT) → San Francisco (SFO)  5:05 PM → 10:40 AM
Jul 29 UA649  San Francisco (SFO) → Tampa (TPA)      9:13 PM → Jul 30, 5:38 AM

⚡ Still to Book

  • Studio Ghibli Museum — Tickets via Lawson Ticket, released June 10 at 10:00 AM JST. Sell out within minutes. ¥1,000/adult. Passport number required at booking.
  • Nintendo Museum (Uji/Kyoto) — Lottery system. Enter April 1–30 at museum-tickets.nintendo.com with a free Nintendo Account. Bring passports on the day.
  • Kumano Kodo Minshuku — Book via tb-kumano.jp/en. July fills months in advance. Email them directly for group bookings.
  • Hokkaido Ryokan — Zaborin or Jozankei Tsuruga. Book 4–6 months ahead. Dinner and breakfast included.
  • Domestic Flight HND→CTS — ANA or JAL, Tokyo → Sapporo. Book now for July peak pricing (~$100–150/person if early).
  • JR Pass (21-day): skip it — Current price is ¥100,000/person (~$665), NOT ¥70,000. Your route's individual tickets total ~¥72,000/person, making the pass a net loss of ~¥28,000/person (¥168,000 for 6). Buy point-to-point via SmartEX app or Klook instead.

IC Cards (Suica)

Each person needs a Suica card. Load ¥5,000–8,000 each. Works on Tokyo Metro, Kyoto buses, local trains, many convenience stores. Buy at airport vending machines on arrival.

SmartEX App

Download before departure. Register a foreign credit card. Book all shinkansen seats digitally — QR code boarding, English interface, small discounts on some routes.

Pocket Wi-Fi / eSIM

Rent a shared pocket Wi-Fi at the airport or buy Japan eSIMs before departure. For 6 people, one pocket Wi-Fi device is usually more economical.

Luggage Forwarding (Takkyubin)

Use Yamato Transport to ship heavy bags between cities. ¥1,500–2,500/bag. Hotels and convenience stores handle pickup. Essential for Kumano Kodo days — walk with daypacks only.

Cash

Japan remains partially cash-driven. Withdraw at 7-Eleven ATMs (most reliable for foreign cards). Budget ¥50,000–80,000/person in cash for the trip.

Restaurant Reservations

Book Kyoto kaiseki dinners (Kikunoi, Hyotei) 2–3 months ahead. Use Tableall or email directly. For Tokyo fine dining (Tempura Kondo), 1–2 months. Concierge at your machiya host can often help.

Practical Logistics

On the Plane & At the Airport

Granular tips from experienced Japan travelers — the kind of detail that saves real time on arrival day.

✈ On the Plane — Forms

Save your accommodation names and phone numbers on your phone before boarding. You'll need them for the mandatory disembarkation and customs cards — filling them out mid-flight is far easier than hunting through email at immigration.

🔌 Type A Adapter

Most Japanese hotels and Airbnbs have two-pronged outlets only. If any of your devices have a third grounding pin, bring a Type A travel adapter. Japan runs on 100V/50–60Hz — charge speeds may be slightly slower than home.

💴 Emergency Cash — Before You Land

Carry approximately ¥10,000 per week of the trip as backup cash. Japan is still significantly cash-heavy — many temples, smaller restaurants, and rural spots are cash-only. Don't rely on card acceptance outside major cities.

🏧 Airport ATMs — Turn Left

After clearing customs at Narita or Haneda, turn left at the "Tokyo Tokyo" welcome wall to find 7-Eleven ATMs. These accept the widest range of foreign cards, offer multi-language menus, and display all fees transparently in your home currency. The most reliable cash source in Japan.

📱 SIM / Pocket Wi-Fi — Turn Right

Turn right at the welcome wall for SIM card and pocket Wi-Fi rental shops — two side-by-side, pick the shorter queue. If using an eSIM, skip entirely; activate it before landing. For a group of 6, one shared pocket Wi-Fi is usually the most cost-effective option.

ℹ️ Lost? Walk Straight

Just past customs, walking straight ahead leads to an Information kiosk staffed by excellent English speakers. If anything about transit, accommodation, or navigation is unclear on arrival, start here before guessing.

🚆 Train into Tokyo from Haneda

From Haneda, take the Keikyu Line (Terminal 2 basement) — buy a ticket to Shinagawa Station (~35 min, ~¥310), a major hub that connects to the Yamanote Line and the rest of the city. Alternatively, the Tokyo Monorail runs to Hamamatsucho Station (~20 min). Use your freshly withdrawn cash; IC cards may not be loaded yet. From Shinagawa, hop the Yamanote Line in any direction.

🎫 IC Cards — Current Status

Standard Suica and Pasmo visitor card sales have been temporarily suspended. Look instead for the Pasmo Passport (¥2,000, available at airport and major stations) — it functions identically. Alternatively, buy individual paper tickets at station machines using cash. Keep paper tickets — you must insert them again to exit at your destination.

On the Ground

Navigation, Money & Etiquette

The things that make daily Japan life work smoothly for a first-time visitor.

🚇 Stick to Trains

Trains are clean, punctual, and dramatically more relaxing than navigating traffic and parking in Japanese cities. For inter-city travel, shinkansen. For cities, subway + JR lines + IC card tap. Taxis and rental cars are for specific situations only (Hokkaido countryside, late-night returns).

🎴 IC Card — Don't Overthink It

Suica, Pasmo, and Icoca all work on the same tap-and-go system in all major cities. Don't stress over which one — get whichever is available and load ¥5,000–8,000. It works on subways, JR local trains, buses, taxis, and most convenience stores.

🧳 Travel Light

Japan is not built for large suitcases. Many of Tokyo's busiest stations (Shibuya, Shinjuku) have long staircases, crowded platforms, and minimal escalators. A backpack + one soft bag is ideal. If you must bring large luggage, use Yamato Transport takkyubin to ship bags directly between hotels — approximately ¥1,500–2,000 per bag per day's travel.

🚌 No Large Bags on City Buses

Large suitcases are not permitted on city buses in Japan. This applies especially to Kyoto, where buses are the primary transit for temple neighborhoods. If you have bulky luggage on a bus day, either send it ahead or use the subway instead.

💰 Keep ¥10,000 in Your Wallet

While Japan is no longer strictly cash-only, keep ¥10,000 accessible at all times. Temples, smaller restaurants (especially those using meal-ticket vending machines), rural shops, and some izakayas still require cash. 7-Eleven ATMs and Japan Post Office ATMs accept foreign cards reliably. Avoid other ATMs — acceptance rates vary.

🪙 Coin Consolidation Trick

Japan uses coins heavily (¥1, ¥5, ¥10, ¥50, ¥100, ¥500). When your pocket fills with change, go to a 7-Eleven, grab a snack, and dump all loose coins into the self-checkout machine's coin reservoir — it pays for your items and returns the minimum number of coins. Genuinely useful.

📲 Essential Apps

Download before departure: Google Maps (transit directions, offline maps), Google Translate (camera mode for menus and signs), Japan Transit Planner (Navitime) for detailed rail routing, SmartEX for shinkansen reservations. Also: Tabelog for restaurant discovery and ratings.

🗑️ Carry a Trash Bag

Public rubbish bins are extremely rare in Japan — a direct consequence of the 1995 Aum Shinrikyo sarin attacks, after which most public bins were removed and never returned. Carry a small plastic bag for rubbish. Convenience stores have bins at the counter for items purchased there; use them. Return everything else to your hotel.

Etiquette

Behavior in Public

Japan's public culture is governed by consideration for others — not rules exactly, but strong social expectations. The simplest guide: observe the locals and follow their lead.

🤫 Trains Are Quiet Spaces

Keep voices low on all public transit. Phone calls are strongly discouraged — most Japanese people use silent mode and text rather than call on trains. Loud conversations in English (or any language) are a genuine irritant to other passengers. This applies especially on the shinkansen.

🍜 Eating While Walking

Eating while walking is generally frowned upon in Japan except at specific markets (Tsukiji, Nishiki, festival stalls) where it is expected and acceptable. At temples and shrines, eating in the precincts is inappropriate unless there is a designated food area. When in doubt, stop, eat, and continue.

♨️ Onsen Etiquette

Wash thoroughly at the shower stations before entering the communal bath. No swimwear. No phones or cameras in the bath area. Tattoos are prohibited at most traditional onsen — check policy in advance for your Hokkaido ryokan. Tie up long hair. Enter quietly and don't splash.

⛩️ At Temples & Shrines

Speak quietly. Photography is generally permitted in grounds but check for signs before photographing interiors or specific objects. Some temple interiors prohibit photography entirely (Sanjusangendo, for example). Bow slightly when passing through torii gates. Don't touch sacred objects or step on threshold stones.

🥢 Chopstick Rules

Don't stick chopsticks upright in rice (resembles funeral incense offerings). Don't pass food chopstick-to-chopstick (mirrors a funeral rite). Don't point with chopsticks. Resting them across your bowl between bites is fine; use the chopstick rest when provided.

🌸 The Biggest Advice

Don't stress about seeing everything. Japan rewards presence over checklist completion. The small moments — a bowl of ramen in a neighborhood you weren't planning to visit, a conversation with a machiya host, rain on a temple garden — are often what stays with you longest.

東京
July 9 · Arrival Night

Tokyo I

Arrival night after 24 hours of travel. Drop bags, eat ramen, sleep. The city in its rawest first impression — Sumida, Asakusa, and the Skytree glowing outside the window.

Day-by-Day
Map
Restaurants

Wed–Thu, July 8–9 — Travel Day · Tampa → Tokyo

Day 01
Wednesday July 8 · Leg 1 — Tampa → San Francisco

UA611 · Depart Tampa (TPA) 6:42 AM → Arrive San Francisco (SFO) 9:19 AM. ~1h51m layover at SFO. Confirmation: JCYY32.

Wednesday July 8 · Leg 2 — San Francisco → Osaka (overnight Pacific crossing)

UA35 · Depart San Francisco (SFO) 11:10 AM → Arrive Osaka Kansai (KIX) Thursday July 9, 2:50 PM (~11h40m, crosses the International Date Line — you lose Wednesday night entirely). Sleep on the plane. Noise-cancelling headphones essential.

Thursday July 9 · Leg 3 — Osaka → Tokyo (⚠ Tight Connection)

NH992 (ANA) · Depart Osaka Kansai (KIX) 3:50 PM → Arrive Tokyo Haneda (HND) 5:20 PM. Only 1 hour connection at KIX — this is the critical moment of the trip.

At KIX, you must clear Japanese immigration (first port of entry) before boarding the domestic flight. In July, immigration queues can run 30–60 min. United/ANA sold this connection as valid, so if UA35 is delayed, NH992 should be held or you'll be automatically rebooked — but move fast off the plane. Bags are checked through to HND; you should not need to collect them at KIX.

Thursday July 9 · Arrive Tokyo Haneda (HND) 5:20 PM

Clear any remaining customs at HND. Pick up Suica / Pasmo Passport cards at airport vending machines (load ¥5,000–8,000 each). Take the Keikyu Line from T3 basement toward central Tokyo (~35 min to Shinagawa, ¥310). From Shinagawa, Yamanote Line north to Akihabara, then Sobu Line east to Kinshicho — the Sumida Airbnb is a short walk (~50 min total). Or take a taxi (~¥4,500–6,000, ~40 min).

Tonight is a recovery night. Walk the neighborhood, eat ramen, sleep early. You've been traveling for ~24 hours. The city will wait.

Accommodation · Tonight

Sumida Airbnb — 3-12-3 Taihei, Sumida City. Oshiage/Asakusa area, a 10-min walk from Senso-ji and the Tokyo Skytree. One night before the shinkansen to Kyoto tomorrow morning.

📦 Tonight — Ship Bags to Kyoto (Yamato Transport)
Drop heavy suitcases at a 7-Eleven near the Sumida Airbnb tonight — the Sumida area has several within walking distance. Look for the black cat (クロネコ) logo in the window. Address your bags to the Kyoto machiya (get the Japanese address from Google Maps before you go). Drop off before 6–7 PM for next-day delivery — bags will arrive at the machiya the following day while you're on the shinkansen.
  • Suitcases need only a luggage tag (no repackaging needed)
  • Fill out the delivery slip at the counter — staff will help; write the destination address in Japanese
  • Payment in cash at drop-off
  • Bags will be waiting at the machiya when you arrive — travel tomorrow with daypacks only
Tokyo in Context Edo — Tokyo's former name — became Japan's administrative capital in 1603 under Tokugawa Ieyasu. It was renamed Tokyo ("Eastern Capital") in 1869 when Emperor Meiji relocated from Kyoto. The city was rebuilt twice in the 20th century: after the 1923 Great Kantō Earthquake and after the firebombing of 1945. This double reconstruction explains Tokyo's layered, often disorienting urban fabric — ancient shrine beside glass tower. See Columbia Asia for Educators on the Edo period and World History Encyclopedia on Edo Japan.
Get Suica / Pasmo Passport at HNDBags checked through TPA→HND — no pickup at KIXEarly sleep — beat the jetlag
Itinerary Map

Tokyo — Key Sites

Numbered markers for all key itinerary sites across our Tokyo days — temples, museums, markets, and shopping destinations.

Where to Eat

Tokyo — Restaurants

Tokyo has more Michelin-starred restaurants than any other city on earth. These are the ones that earn their place on this itinerary — from rigorous high-end to cash-only neighborhood treasures.

Michelin 2★
Tempura Kondo
Ginza · Tempura · ¥15,000–25,000

Chef Fumio Kondo's precise, nearly weightless tempura — the finest expression of the form in Tokyo. The seasonal vegetable courses are as important as the seafood. Reserve 1–2 months ahead. See Michelin entry for current status.

Michelin 3★
Sézanne
Marunouchi · French · ¥30,000–50,000

Chef Daniel Calvert's French cuisine in the Four Seasons Marunouchi. Exceptional tasting menu built around Japanese seasonal ingredients. Tokyo's most acclaimed Western kitchen. Reserve well in advance through the hotel concierge.

Michelin Bib Gourmand
Tonkatsu Narikura
Shibuya · Tonkatsu · ¥2,500–4,500

#1 on Tabelog. Pork from four heritage breeds, fried one slice at a time. Queue 1–2 hours; arrive at opening (11 AM) or 2 PM. The gold standard for tonkatsu in the city.

Michelin Bib Gourmand
Grill GRAND
Asakusa (near Sensoji) · Yoshoku · ¥1,800–2,800

Est. 1941. The omuhayashi — rice dome draped in egg and a demi-glace that takes two weeks to prepare — is a Tokyo institution. Cash only. Perfect after the morning at Sensoji.

Michelin Bib Gourmand
Tamawarai
Omotesando · Soba · ¥2,000–3,500

Home-grown buckwheat — they cultivate their own grain and mill in-house. The freshest soba in Tokyo, in a beautifully spare setting. Bib Gourmand for years. Ideal final-night dinner.

Michelin Bib Gourmand
Negima
Shinjuku · Izakaya · ¥3,000–5,000

Eight seats. Chef/owner serves in a traditional kimono; sake poured old-style. A living Meiji-era atmosphere. Cash only. Book in advance — among the most sought-after atmospheric dining experiences in Tokyo.

Local Favorite
Ichiran Ramen
Multiple locations · Tonkotsu · ¥1,000–1,500

Solo-booth ramen — you order on a slip of paper and your bowl arrives through a curtain. Theatrical, delicious, kid-approved. Good for arrival night when nobody wants to make decisions.

Local Favorite
Tsukiji Outer Market Stalls
Tsukiji · Seafood · ¥500–2,000

Not a restaurant but essential. Fresh tamagoyaki on a stick, tuna sashimi, uni, tamago-don at the counter stalls. The best ¥1,500 lunch in Tokyo. Go before 1 PM.

Local Favorite
Depachika (Mitsukoshi Ginza B2)
Ginza · Food Hall · ¥800–3,000

The basement food floors of Ginza Mitsukoshi and Matsuya represent Japanese food culture at its most refined retail form. Buy prepared foods, exceptional wagashi, and all food souvenirs here.

⭐ Chef Recommendations — Tiffani Faison, Pagu Boston
Chef Rec · Soba
Tsukiji Soba Academy — Cooking Class with Akila Inouye
Tsukiji · Cooking Class · ¥8,000–15,000

A hands-on soba-making class with master Akila Inouye at the Tsukiji Soba Academy. One of the most respected soba education experiences in Tokyo — you learn to mill, knead, cut, and cook your own buckwheat noodles from scratch. A meaningful engagement with craft rather than just consumption. soba-akademy.com

Chef Rec · Should be 1★
Ukiyo
Tokyo · Contemporary Japanese · ¥15,000–25,000

Tiffani's rec: should be a Michelin star. Intimate, ingredient-driven contemporary Japanese cuisine with an aesthetic seriousness that exceeds its official recognition. A discovery rather than a landmark.

Chef Rec · Michelin 1★
Kabi
Meguro · Fermentation-forward · ¥15,000–20,000

One Michelin Star. Chef Kenji Kamo's fermentation-driven cuisine — koji-aged proteins, house-fermented vinegars, extraordinary vegetable treatments. One of Tokyo's most intellectually interesting tasting menus. Reserve well ahead at restaurant-kabi.com.

Chef Rec · Michelin 2★
Narisawa
Minami-Aoyama · Innovative Satoyama · ¥30,000–45,000

Chef Yoshihiro Narisawa's "Innovative Satoyama Cuisine" — a tasting menu built around Japan's forest-to-table ecology. Regularly appears on the World's 50 Best list. One of the most conceptually rigorous dining experiences in Asia. Reserve months ahead at narisawa-yoshihiro.com.

Chef Rec · Michelin 3★
L'Effervescence
Nishi-Azabu · French-Japanese · ¥35,000–50,000

Three Michelin Stars. Chef Shinobu Namae studied under Michel Bras in France and Fergus Henderson in London — the synthesis is a profoundly Japan-grounded tasting menu that uses European technique to illuminate Japanese ingredients. Reserve at leffervescence.jp.

Chef Rec · Historic Soba
Kanda Matsuya
Kanda · Traditional Soba · ¥1,200–2,500

Tiffani's rec. One of Tokyo's oldest soba shops — established in the Meiji era, unchanged in character. Hand-cut zaru soba in cold dashi. The antithesis of the trendy restaurant. Go for lunch on a weekday. No reservations; expect a short queue.

Chef Rec · World Pizza
Pizza Studio Tamaki (PST)
Meguro · Neapolitan Pizza · ¥2,500–4,500

Named to the World's 50 Best Pizzas list. Chef Tamaki trained in Naples; the result is arguably the finest Neapolitan pizza outside Italy — blistered, chewy, with exceptional ingredient sourcing. Excellent for a lunch or casual evening. Queue or book ahead.

📰 Condé Nast + Online Recommendations
Condé Nast Pick · Ramen
Ginza Hachigou
Ginza · Ramen · ¥1,500–2,000

Condé Nast recommended. A refined take on ramen in the most upscale neighborhood of Tokyo — the setting and bowl quality both exceed expectations. Full Condé Nast Tokyo food guide. Reserve or arrive at opening.

Ramen Pilgrimage
Shin-Yokohama Ramen Museum
Shin-Yokohama · Ramen Museum · ¥380 entry

An underground recreation of 1950s Tokyo housing 9 ramen restaurants from across Japan — each representing a regional style. Not a single bowl but an education in Japanese ramen geography. Entry ¥380; bowls ¥800–1,200 each. 20 min from Tokyo by shinkansen. raumen.co.jp

Ramen Street
Tokyo Station Ramen Street
Tokyo Station B1 · Ramen · ¥1,000–1,800

8 celebrated ramen shops concentrated in Tokyo Station's basement — the most convenient concentration of quality ramen in the city. Includes Rokurinsha (tsukemen), Soranoiro (vegetarian options), and seasonal specials. Arrive at 11 AM or after 2 PM to avoid peak queues.

Condé Nast Pick · Ramen
Ichiban / Shaniya
Various Tokyo locations · Ramen · ¥1,000–1,800

Condé Nast recommended local ramen spots. Ichiban is known for a rich tonkotsu-shoyu hybrid broth; Shaniya for a lighter, clear chicken-based style. Good neighborhood alternatives to the tourist-heavy flagship destinations.

Standing Sushi
Uogashi Nihon-ichi (Shibuya Dogenzaka)
Shibuya · Standing Sushi · ¥1,000–2,000

One of the best standing sushi bars in Tokyo — watch the chefs work at close range, order from an English menu or point at the counter. Fast, excellent, and genuinely local. Multiple locations (Kyobashi, Akihabara, Kichijoji, Asakusabashi, Akasaka). The Dogenzaka branch is freshly reopened. Video guide

Temple Food
Fukagawa Fudo-do Temple Precinct
Monzen-Nakacho · Temple Food & Market · ¥500–1,500

Condé Nast rec. The area around Fukagawa Fudo-do temple (Shingon sect, founded 1703) has a cluster of traditional food stalls and shops serving fukagawa-meshi (clam and rice — the historic dish of the area) and temple confections. A non-touristy way to combine Buddhist site-visiting with neighborhood food culture.

Food Hall
Ginza Mitsukoshi Depachika (B2 & B3)
Ginza · Department Store Food Hall · ¥500–5,000

B2: an entire floor of ready-made food, Japanese and Western sweets, baked goods. B3: in-house supermarket with one of the city's most impressive cheese counters and extraordinary Japanese fruit. Refrigerated coin lockers available — store perishables while you continue shopping. One of the finest retail food environments in Asia.

🍸 Bars
High End
The Bar at Ritz-Carlton Tokyo
Roppongi · Hotel Bar · ¥2,500–6,000/drink

Recommended for the view and the whisky list. The Ritz-Carlton Tokyo's 45th-floor bar has an extraordinary collection of Japanese whisky (Yamazaki, Hibiki, Hakushu vintage expressions) and a panoramic Tokyo skyline view. Worth one visit for the elevation and the bar program.

Unique Experience
Twillo Bar Cart
Various Tokyo locations · Mobile Bar

A unique Tokyo concept — a mobile bar cart that travels through the city. Follow their social for current location. Craft cocktails, small-batch spirits, and the best way to have a drink while walking through Shibuya or Shinjuku at night.

東京
July 14–15 · Two Days

Tokyo II

Back in the capital after four days in Kyoto — Meiji Shrine, the Imperial Palace East Gardens, Sensoji, the Tokyo National Museum, and the Ghibli Museum before the Hakone day trip.

Day-by-Day
Map
Restaurants

Tuesday, July 14 — Return · Meiji Shrine · Imperial Palace East Gardens

Day 06
Morning · Shinkansen Kyoto → Tokyo

Morning Hikari (2h45m, SmartEX). Arrive by midday. Check in, drop bags.

Afternoon · Meiji Jingu (明治神宮)

Meiji Shrine was established 1920 to commemorate Emperor Meiji. The forested approach — 100,000 trees from across Japan, planted by 100,000 volunteers — is designed, not found, and the design is the argument: state sacrality as engineered nature. Read it as the Meiji reinvention of Shinto as civic religion, extending the shinbutsu bunri theme from Kumano and Seiganto-ji into the modern period. Free entry. See World History Encyclopedia on State Shinto and Encyclopedia of Shinto on Meiji Shrine.

Meiji Jingu · Free · 5 min from Harajuku Station
Late Afternoon · Imperial Palace East Gardens

The Imperial Palace East Gardens (Higashi Gyoen) occupy the former Edo Castle site — the physical anchor for reading Tokyo as a castle-city that became an imperial capital. The main keep burned in 1657 and was never rebuilt; the stone foundations remain. Walking this topography you read: Tokugawa statecraft (1603–1868), Meiji imperial reappropriation (1869), and postwar public access (opened 1968). Free, open Tue–Sun. See Columbia Asia for Educators on Tokugawa Edo and World History Encyclopedia on Edo Castle.

Imperial Palace East Gardens · Free · Tue–Sun · Otemachi Station
Evening · Sushi Dinner

Good evening for a proper sushi dinner — Tsukiji Sushisay or Sushi no Midori. Tomorrow is the longest cultural day of the Tokyo stay.

Accommodation · July 14–16

Mic House Shinjuku Airbnb — 1-17-8 Nishiochiai, Shinjuku-ku. Three nights here. 15 min walk or short bus to Shinjuku Station. Good base for the Hakone day trip (July 16) and the early Haneda departure on July 17 (Haneda ~40 min by train from Shinjuku).

🏨 Gupta Family — Hyatt Centric Ginza Tokyo · July 13–17
6-6-7 Ginza, Chuo-ku, Tokyo 104-0061 · Tel: +81 3 6837 1234
They arrive a day before us (July 13, while we're still in Kyoto) and check out July 17 (the morning we fly to Hokkaido). Good overlap July 14–16 — Ginza is 30 min from Shinjuku by subway, easy to coordinate dinners or day-trip meetups.

Wednesday, July 15 — Sensoji, Tokyo National Museum & Ghibli

Day 07
Morning · Sensoji, Asakusa

Sensoji (founded 628 AD) is Tokyo's oldest Buddhist temple and its most visited. Arrive at 7:30–8 AM before crowds. The main hall is consecrated to Kannon Bosatsu (Avalokiteśvara, Bodhisattva of Compassion) — a golden image said to have been found in the Sumida River by two fishermen, and never displayed publicly in over 1,000 years. The omikuji fortune-draw ritual is worth doing with the kids.

For the iconography of Kannon in Japanese Buddhism, see The Zen Site and Sacred Texts Buddhist Archive. The distinction between the historical Buddha and bodhisattva figures like Kannon is central to Mahāyāna doctrine — see Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy on Japanese Zen for the philosophical grounding.

Sensoji and AsakusaFounded in 628 under Empress Suiko, predating Edo by nearly a millennium. The current structures date to postwar reconstruction (1958) — the originals were destroyed in the March 1945 firebombing. The temple belongs to the Tendai sect, which also transmitted esoteric Buddhist practice (mikkyō) to Japan from Tang China in the 9th century.
Afternoon · Tokyo National Museum

The Tokyo National Museum in Ueno Park is the world's largest collection of Japanese art — 110,000+ objects. For this trip's focus, prioritize: Room 11 (Honkan) — Buddhist sculpture from Heian and Kamakura periods, including extraordinary wooden statues of the type you'll encounter throughout Kyoto. Room 13 — lacquerwork, including examples of the maki-e tradition relevant to Kodai-ji. The TNM's online collection database is searchable in English. Entry ¥1,000; free under 18.

Also in the Ueno Zone · Yanaka · Ameyoko · Kappabashi

Yanaka (谷中) survived Tokyo's double 20th-century reconstruction intact — neither the 1923 earthquake nor the 1945 firebombing destroyed it. Walking Yanaka gives you the closest thing to Edo shitamachi grain: narrow lanes, wooden merchant houses, temple cemeteries. The cemetery (Yanaka Reien) contains graves of many Meiji-era figures. Ten minutes on foot from TNM. Ameyoko market runs under the tracks between Ueno and Okachimachi — a post-WWII black market turned legitimate street market. Dense, loud, local.

Kappabashi Kitchen Town — 170+ professional kitchen supply shops, 15 min west of Sensoji. Landmark knife shop: Kama-asa (est. 1908). Also copper pots, donabe clay pots, lacquerware, ceramics, and the famous sampuru plastic food display models (a specifically Japanese commercial form developed in the 1920s).

Yanaka · Free · 10 min from TNMKappabashi · Kama-asa knives · 15 min from SensojiAmeyoko · Street snacks · Ueno
Late Afternoon · Studio Ghibli Museum

JR Chuo Line from Ueno to Mitaka (40 min). The Ghibli Museum was designed by Hayao Miyazaki personally — no prescribed route, no maps, "let's get lost together." The Saturn Theater shows exclusive short films. Allow 2 hours. Tickets via Lawson Ticket released June 10 at 10 AM JST.

Book Ghibli June 10 at 10AM JSTSensoji · Free · Open 24hrTNM Ueno · ¥1,000
Accommodation · Tonight

Mic House Shinjuku Airbnb — 1-17-8 Nishiochiai, Shinjuku-ku. Early night recommended: Romancecar departs Shinjuku at 7 AM tomorrow.

🏨 Gupta Family — Hyatt Centric Ginza Tokyo · Still in Tokyo (July 15)
They're at the Hyatt Centric Ginza through July 17. Consider coordinating dinner tonight — Tsukiji / Ginza area is their backyard and 30 min from Shinjuku by subway.
Itinerary Map

Tokyo II — Key Sites

Meiji Shrine, Imperial Palace, Sensoji, Tokyo National Museum, Ghibli Museum, and the Shinjuku Airbnb base.

Where to Eat

Tokyo II — Restaurants

The full Tokyo restaurant list applies — Tsukiji, Meiji-area dining, and coordination with the Guptas at Hyatt Centric Ginza.

See the Tokyo I restaurants tab for the full Tokyo dining list — all options apply across both visits.

P · M · S · D · I
京都
July 10–13 · Four Days

Kyoto I

Japan's ancient capital and the center of this trip's Buddhist and Zen itinerary. Four days in the city's eastern and western districts, plus the Nintendo Museum in Uji.

Day-by-Day
Map
Restaurants

Friday, July 10 — Tokyo → Kyoto

Day 02
Morning · Shinkansen Hikari — Tokyo Station → Kyoto

Walk or take the Sobu Line from the Sumida Airbnb to Tokyo Station (~20 min). Board the Shinkansen Hikari — aim for a 7:30–9:00 AM departure. Duration: ~2h40m. Book via SmartEX app (~¥13,000/person, ~¥78,000 for six). Reserve seats on the E side (right side facing forward) — around Shin-Fuji and Mishima Stations (~40 min in), you may see Fuji on a clear morning.

The Hikari stops at Kyoto; the faster Nozomi skips it on some services — confirm when booking. Arrive Kyoto Station by midday.

Midday · Check-In to the Machiya

Check in to the Kyoto machiya — a traditional wooden townhouse near Nijo Castle, Nakagyo-ku. Booked via kyoto-machiya-inn.com; properties include Kiyu, Natsume-an, or Kohaku-an. Tatami rooms, lattice facade, small courtyard. 15 min walk to Nijo Castle, 20 min by bus to Gion. Drop bags, get oriented, stretch your legs.

Afternoon · First Kyoto Immersion

Nishiki Market ("Kyoto's Kitchen") — a covered market running five blocks east from Teramachi. Fresh tofu skin, pickled vegetables, grilled skewers, matcha soft-serve. A good first hour in the city. Then walk east toward Gion in the late afternoon — Hanamikoji-dori at dusk, when the ochaya (tea houses) are lighting up, is one of the most evocative streets in Japan.

Evening · Dinner

Good first dinner options near the machiya or Gion: Omen (thick udon in dashi, Gion location — casual, excellent) or the unforgettable Menbakaichidai ("fire ramen" — the bowl is set on fire tableside; a Kyoto original and a great conversation-starter with the kids).

Accommodation · All Four Nights (July 10–13)

Kyoto machiya via kyoto-machiya-inn.com — Kiyu, Natsume-an, or Kohaku-an. Nakagyo-ku, near Nijo Castle. Traditional townhouse for 6, tatami rooms, courtyard. Hosts can help with restaurant reservations.

Saturday, July 11 — Kodai-ji & Nintendo Museum

Day 03
Morning · Kodai-ji Temple

Kodai-ji was founded in 1606 by Nene (Kita no Mandokoro), wife of Toyotomi Hideyoshi, as a memorial to her husband. The Otama-ya mausoleum interiors are decorated in the Kodai-ji maki-e style — gold lacquerwork on black ground — considered the finest example of Momoyama decorative arts. Two tea houses designed by Sen no Rikyu's tradition survive here. The bamboo grove predates Arashiyama's by centuries.

Momoyama Aesthetics & LacquerThe Momoyama period (1568–1615) produced Japan's most opulent decorative arts — a brief explosive reaction against centuries of Zen restraint. The maki-e lacquerwork at Kodai-ji represents the period's zenith. For the relationship between Zen aesthetic philosophy and the tea ceremony tradition that frames Kodai-ji, see The Zen Site on Zen Art and the Metropolitan Museum essay on Momoyama period art.
Afternoon · Nintendo Museum (Uji)

Kintetsu Kyoto Line to Ogura Station (30 min). Nintendo Museum is built inside Nintendo's former Uji Ogura Plant, where they manufactured Hanafuda playing cards beginning in 1889. The museum traces the full arc from cards through Game Boy to Switch, with 8 interactive exhibits using 10 digital coins. The Hanafuda workshop (reserve same-day at the info desk) lets you make your own cards — a genuine connection to the pre-game origin of the company. See official Nintendo Museum site for current hours. Tickets via lottery, April 1–30. Bring passports — only valid ID for non-Japanese nationals.

While in Uji: the town is one of Japan's primary matcha-growing regions. Try matcha soft-serve and sweets at Nakamura Tokichi (Bib Gourmand), and walk along the Uji River — the setting of one of the final chapters of The Tale of Genji.

Kodai-ji · ¥600 · Arrive 9 AMNintendo Museum · Lottery ticketsNakamura Tokichi · Bib Gourmand · Uji

Sunday, July 12 — Arashiyama & Otagi Nenbutsu-ji

Day 04
Early Morning · Tenryu-ji (Arrive at Opening 8:30 AM)

Tenryu-ji is the most important Rinzai Zen temple in Arashiyama and one of the UNESCO-listed historic monuments of Kyoto. The garden was designed by Musō Soseki (1275–1351) in 1339 — one of the greatest garden designers in Japanese history. It uses shakkei ("borrowed scenery"), integrating the Arashiyama mountains as the garden's backdrop. The pond-stroll style it established became a template for Japanese garden design for centuries. See Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy on Japanese Zen for Musō's position in the transmission of Zen aesthetics, and Dogen's writings on mountains and rivers for the philosophical context of Zen landscape.

Musō Soseki and the Zen Garden TraditionMusō Soseki was simultaneously a Zen master, poet, garden designer, and political advisor to multiple shoguns. His garden at Tenryu-ji embodies the Zen concept that the natural landscape is itself a dharma teaching — what he called "mountains and rivers without end." He founded or restored over 100 Zen temples. See The Zen Site for primary source materials on the Rinzai tradition he advanced.
Mid-Morning · Otagi Nenbutsu-ji

25-minute walk or short bus north of the bamboo grove. Otagi Nenbutsu-ji contains 1,200 stone rakan (arhat — disciples of the historical Buddha) carved by 10,000 volunteers under master sculptor Kōchō Nishimura between 1981–1991. Each face is unique — laughing, grimacing, serene, holding sake, weeping. The rakan iconography in East Asian Buddhism represents those who have achieved liberation but remain in the world to teach. See Sacred Texts Buddhist Archive for the foundational texts on arhat doctrine. This site is one of the most unusual encounters between formal Buddhist iconography and folk devotional creativity in modern Japan.

Tenryu-ji · ¥1,000 · Arrive at openingOtagi Nenbutsu-ji · ¥500 · Unmissable

Monday, July 13 — Zen Core: Ryoan-ji, Kinkaku-ji, Daitoku-ji

Day 05
Morning · Ryoan-ji Rock Garden

Ryōan-ji's karesansui (dry landscape) garden contains 15 stones in raked white gravel — from any single viewpoint, one stone is always hidden. Created in the late 15th or early 16th century (authorship disputed), the garden's meaning has never been officially explained. Arrive at opening (8 AM) and sit for at least 20 minutes in silence before commentary arrives. The garden embodies mu (nothingness) and ma (negative space) — see The Record of Rinzai (Rinzai-roku) for the Rinzai Zen concepts that frame this aesthetic vocabulary, and The Zen Site's main study resources for a scholarly framework.

Karesansui and Zen AestheticsThe dry rock garden tradition derives from the Chinese concept of representing water through stone and sand — a practice that crossed into Japan with Zen Buddhism in the 12th–13th centuries. Ryōan-ji's garden is the most analyzed in the world: interpretations include a tiger crossing water, islands in an ocean, the infinity of the universe, and the experience of kenshō (insight). None is official. For Zen aesthetics and the concept of mu, see the Stanford Encyclopedia.
Afternoon · Daitoku-ji Complex — The Most Important Stop

Daitoku-ji is a working Rinzai Zen monastery with 24 sub-temples. Six are open to the public. This is the most intellectually significant Buddhist destination in Kyoto for a scholar.

Daisen-in: miniature garden of extraordinary symbolic density — every stone encodes a narrative journey through life and death. One of the finest gardens in Japan. Kōtō-in: moss garden of absolute tranquility. Ryōgen-in: five distinct gardens in one, including the smallest rock garden in Japan. The compound has deep connections to Rinzai Zen and to Sen no Rikyu, who built his private tea house here and is buried in the Jukō-in sub-temple. For the historical relationship between Daitoku-ji, the daimyo patrons, and the development of the tea ceremony, see the Met's essay on the tea ceremony in Japan.

Daisen-in · ¥400 · Priority visitKoto-in · ¥600 · Most beautiful garden
📦 Tonight — Ship Bags to Tokyo Shinjuku (Yamato Transport)
Drop bags at a 7-Eleven near the Kyoto machiya tonight — look for the black cat (クロネコ) logo. Address them to the Mic House Shinjuku Airbnb (1-17-8 Nishiochiai, Shinjuku-ku — write in Japanese). Drop off before 6–7 PM for next-day delivery; bags will be waiting at the Shinjuku Airbnb when you arrive tomorrow afternoon.
  • Travel tomorrow on the shinkansen with daypacks only
  • Fill out the delivery slip at the counter — write the destination address in Japanese (Google Maps gives the Japanese address)
  • Payment in cash · Suitcases just need a luggage tag
  • The machiya host can help if you're unsure — they deal with this routinely
Itinerary Map

Kyoto I — Key Sites

Key sites across eastern and western Kyoto, plus Uji. Numbered in roughly the order of our visits.

Where to Eat

Kyoto I — Restaurants

Historic Restaurants

Historic · 400+ Years
Warajiya (わらじや)
Near Sanjusangendo · Keihan Shichijo area · Unagi · ¥4,000–10,000

One of Kyoto's oldest restaurants, serving freshwater eel (unagi) for over 400 years. Near Keihan Shichijo Station — ideal pairing with a Sanjusangendo visit up the road. Mentioned in Tanizaki's In Praise of Shadows as an example of traditional Japanese interior aesthetics. Eating here is a direct encounter with a text on your reading list. Reserve ahead.

Michelin & Curated

Kyoto cuisine (Kyo-ryōri) is among the world's most refined food cultures — restrained, seasonal, philosophically grounded in the same aesthetic values as Zen. Kaiseki is its highest expression.

Michelin 3★
Hyotei
Okazaki · Kaiseki · ¥12,000–30,000

16th-generation family restaurant specializing in asa-gayu (morning rice gruel kaiseki). Full kaiseki at dinner. Extraordinary seasonal precision. Reserve 2–3 months ahead by emailing directly. See the Michelin entry for current details.

Michelin 2★
Kikunoi Honten
Higashiyama · Kaiseki · ¥15,000–25,000

The flagship of the Kikunoi group. Chef Murata Yoshihiro wrote the defining text on Kyoto cuisine. The seasonal omakase here is a measured, deeply traditional expression of what kaiseki can be at its most considered.

Michelin 1★ (accessible branch)
Kikunoi Roan
Higashiyama · Kaiseki · ¥8,000–15,000

The more accessible Kikunoi branch — proper seasonal kaiseki without the full ceremony. Excellent for a family where not everyone wants a two-hour formal dinner. Reserve 2–4 weeks ahead.

Historic Restaurant
Mishima-tei
Nishiki area · Sukiyaki · ¥7,000–15,000

Established 1873. Sukiyaki — thinly sliced wagyu cooked tableside in sweet soy broth — in tatami rooms. One of the most historically grounded dining experiences in the city. Reserve ahead.

Bib Gourmand
Nakamura Tokichi
Uji · Matcha · ¥1,500–3,000

The definitive matcha sweets destination in Japan's primary tea-growing town. Bib Gourmand. Kuzukiri (arrowroot in kuromitsu), matcha parfait, seasonal wagashi. Visit on the Nintendo Museum day in Uji.

Local Favorite
Omen Gion
Gion · Udon · ¥1,500–2,500

Thick handmade udon in a light dashi broth, served in a converted machiya on the edge of Gion. No fuss, consistently excellent, family-friendly. One of Kyoto's best casual restaurants.

Local Favorite
Menbakaichidai
Downtown · Fire Ramen · ¥1,800

The chef sets your bowl ablaze at the table. Theatrical, delicious, and one of the things kids will talk about for years. Queue 30 min before opening or at 2 PM.

Market
Nishiki Market
Nakagyo · Food Market · ¥500–2,000

Kyoto's Kitchen — a 400m covered market, 400 years old, with 100+ stalls. Try yuba (tofu skin), tsukemono (pickles), fresh mochi, tamago skewers, and sesame tofu. Best before 2 PM.

⭐ Chef Recommendations — Tiffani Faison, Pagu Boston
Chef Rec · Michelin 1★
Lurra°
Kyoto · Contemporary · ¥20,000–35,000

Tiffani's top Kyoto pick. One Michelin Star. Chef Tomonori Danzaki's highly personal tasting menu built around exceptional Japanese ingredients and a quiet, focused dining room. One of Kyoto's most considered contemporary restaurants. Reserve well ahead — small room, high demand.

Chef Rec · Breakfast
Lorimer
Kyoto · All-Day Café / Breakfast · ¥1,500–3,500

Tiffani's breakfast recommendation. A beautiful, light-filled café with exceptional coffee and a breakfast menu that bridges Japanese and European sensibilities — housemade bread, seasonal eggs, local produce. One of the best morning meals in Kyoto. No reservations; arrive early.

Chef Rec · Spanish-Japanese
En Boca
Kyoto · Spanish-Japanese / Pizza · ¥3,000–8,000

Tiffani's rec. A rare Kyoto hybrid: Spanish technique meeting Japanese ingredients, plus a standout pizza program. The kind of restaurant that only works because Kyoto's ingredient culture is so strong. Fun, creative, and very good. Reserve ahead.

Chef Rec · If You Can Get In
Monk
Kyoto · Counter Omakase · ¥25,000–40,000

Tiffani's highest-difficulty Kyoto rec — "if you can get in." A tiny omakase counter where the cooking is personal, serious, and almost impossible to book. Start trying months before the trip. No formal website — find via reservation platforms like Tableall or Omakase.

富士
July 16 · Day Trip from Tokyo

Fuji / Hakone

A full day in the Hakone volcanic region — sculpture, sulfur vents, and the most iconic view in Japan.

Day-by-Day
Map
Restaurants

Thursday, July 16 — Hakone & Mt. Fuji

Day 08
Early Morning · Depart for Hakone (7 AM)

Romancecar (Odakyu Line) from Shinjuku to Hakone-Yumoto (85 min, ¥2,470/person — NOT JR Pass). Early departure gives the best odds of clear Fuji views before afternoon cloud cover. July is peak summer — cloud cover after noon is common. Early is essential.

Full Day · Hakone Loop

Hakone Open Air Museum — Japan's premier outdoor sculpture park. Works by Picasso, Rodin, Henry Moore, Giacometti, Taro Okamoto. The setting against forested Hakone mountains is extraordinary. Allow 2 hours. ¥1,600 adults, ¥800 under 15. See the official museum site for current exhibitions.

Owakudani (volcanic valley) — sulfur vents, hot springs, and kuro-tamago (black eggs hard-boiled in sulfuric springs, said to add 7 years to life). The ropeway to/from here offers the best Fuji views in clear conditions.

Lake Ashi — boat cruise on the volcanic caldera lake with Fuji as backdrop. Walk the Sugi Namiki cedar avenue — 500-year-old trees lining the old Tokaido highway.

Mt. Fuji — Sacred GeographyAt 3,776m, Fuji is Japan's highest and most sacred mountain. The word "fuji" may derive from "fuchi," a fire deity of the Ainu. Fuji has been depicted in tens of thousands of works — most famously Hokusai's Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (c.1831). The mountain is still an active stratovolcano; its last eruption was 1707. See UNESCO World Heritage entry for Fujisan (listed as Cultural Heritage in 2013) and World History Encyclopedia on Fuji's religious significance.
Evening · Return to Tokyo

Romancecar back from Hakone-Yumoto to Shinjuku (~85 min). Back by 7–8 PM. Light dinner near Nishiochiai. Early night — tomorrow is the early flight from Haneda to Sapporo. From the Shinjuku Airbnb, Haneda is ~40 min by Keikyu from Shinagawa or ~35 min via Tokyo Monorail from Hamamatsucho. Aim for a 6–7 AM departure so leave the apartment by 4:30 AM.

Accommodation · Tonight

Mic House Shinjuku Airbnb — last night at Nishiochiai before Hokkaido. Set alarms. Confirm your HND→CTS flight time.

Itinerary Map

Fuji / Hakone — Key Sites

The Hakone loop: sculpture museum, volcanic valley, lake. Plus the Fuji summit for orientation.

Where to Eat

Fuji / Hakone — Restaurants

Hakone has limited but quality food options — focus on local specialties and a relaxed meal after a full day.

Hakone Classic
Hakone Soba Tamura
Hakone-Yumoto · Soba · ¥1,500–2,500

Long-running soba shop near Hakone-Yumoto station. Hand-cut buckwheat noodles in a clean dashi broth — the right meal after a volcanic walk. Arrive before noon for best selection.

Local Specialty
Owakudani Kuro-tamago Stand
Owakudani · Eggs · ¥500

The famous black eggs cooked in sulfuric springs — eat them at the viewing area overlooking the volcanic valley. More ritual than cuisine, but part of the Hakone experience.

Shinjuku Return
Tonkatsu Narikura
Shibuya · Bib Gourmand · ¥2,500–4,500

If you can manage the queue on return, this is the best Tokyo tonkatsu — pork from 4 heritage breeds, fried one slice at a time. Arrive at 11 AM or 2 PM.

北海道
July 17–19 · Three Days

Hokkaido

Japan's northern island — vast landscapes, extraordinary seafood, lavender fields, the most dramatic change of pace in the trip, and one night in a ryokan with private rotenburo.

Day-by-Day
Map
Restaurants
Plan A · Sapporo & Furano
Plan B · Daisetsuzan & Mountains

Friday, July 17 — Arrival in Sapporo

Day 09
Morning · Fly Tokyo → Sapporo

ANA or JAL, Haneda to New Chitose Airport (~1h30m). Airport Express to Sapporo Station (37 min, ¥1,150). Check in. Domestic flights are NOT covered by JR Pass — book independently.

Afternoon · Sapporo

Odori Park — 1.5km green axis through central Sapporo. Walk east to west, grab Hokkaido sweet corn or the extraordinary milk soft-serve. Hokkaido Jingu — the principal Shinto shrine of Hokkaido, set in a forested park 15 min from center. See Encyclopedia of Shinto (Kokugakuin University) for the theological structure of shrine worship.

Nijo Market — Fresh sea urchin (uni), crab, salmon roe (ikura), fresh scallops. Buy and eat at stalls. Hokkaido seafood in July is at summer peak.

Accommodation · Tonight (July 17) & July 19

Sapporo Airbnb — Nakajima Park / Susukino area. Two nights in Sapporo bookending the ryokan night (July 18). Central location, walkable to Odori Park, Nijo Market, and the Susukino dining district.

Saturday, July 18 — Furano, Biei & Ryokan Night

Day 10
Full Day · Furano & Biei

Farm Tomita — Japan's most famous lavender farm. July is peak season: the entire valley in full purple bloom. Lavender ice cream, essential oils, and photography conditions that explain why Hokkaido became Japan's aspirational landscape. Arrive at 9 AM for light and thin crowds.

Biei Blue Pond (Aoiike) — An intensely vivid natural blue pond created by aluminum hydroxide in the water, with dead trees rising from the surface. A genuinely improbable landscape. 30 min from Tomita. Late afternoon light is best.

Patchwork Road, Biei — Rolling farmland hills in different crop colors: yellow wheat, green corn, purple lavender, brown earth. The 10-minute circuit from Ken and Mary's Tree viewpoint is essential.

Evening · Ryokan Check-In

Transfer to your Hokkaido ryokan. Arrive in time for the evening kaiseki dinner and first onsen soak.

Accommodation · Tonight

Zaborin (Niseko, Kutchan) — secluded luxury ryokan, private rotenburo in each room, kaiseki dinner using Hokkaido ingredients, minimalist Zen design. ~¥50,000–80,000/person with 2 meals. Book 4–6 months ahead. zaborin.com
Alternative: Jozankei Tsuruga — grand onsen resort in the Toyohira River valley, 60 min from Sapporo. Multiple indoor and outdoor baths, kaiseki dinner included. ~¥30,000–55,000/person. Easier access from central Hokkaido.

Ryokan & Onsen — Historical ContextThe ryokan tradition dates to the 8th century when imperial messengers required lodging along official routes. The format — tatami rooms, futon, yukata robes, kaiseki dinner, communal or private onsen baths — has remained essentially unchanged for over 1,000 years. Hot spring (onsen) culture is intertwined with both Shinto purification ritual and Buddhist ascetic practice. The mineral content of each spring is classified and named; different springs are said to treat different ailments — a tradition documented in texts from the Nara period (8th century). See Encyclopedia of Shinto on misogi (purification) for the ritual bathing tradition.

Sunday, July 19 — Ryokan Morning & Sapporo Art

Day 11
Morning · Rotenburo & Ryokan Breakfast

Wake at 5:30–6 AM for the outdoor rotenburo (hot spring bath) before other guests are up. The morning kaiseki breakfast: miso soup, grilled fish, rice, pickles, tamagoyaki, tofu. Allow 2+ hours before checkout.

Afternoon · Moerenuma Park & Sapporo Beer

Moerenuma Park was designed entirely by sculptor Isamu Noguchi (1904–1988) — the entire landscape is the artwork: glass pyramid, conical hill, vast grass plain, water feature. One of the most important works of land art in Asia. Free entry; rent bikes. See World History Encyclopedia on Noguchi for his biography and artistic context.

Sapporo Beer Museum — Free entry. Sapporo is Japan's oldest beer brand (1876). The exhibition in the red-brick complex is a genuine industrial history. Paid tasting room for Kaitakushi lager and Black Label.

Evening · Fly Back to Kyoto

Take the Airport Express from Sapporo Station to New Chitose Airport (37 min, ¥1,150). Fly CTS → KIX (Osaka Kansai, ~1h45m, ANA/JAL afternoon/evening departures) or CTS → ITM (Osaka Itami, ~1h40m). From Kansai Airport: Haruka Express to Kyoto Station (~75 min, ¥2,850, reserved seat). From Itami: Limousine Bus to Kyoto (~55 min). Arrive at the Kyoto machiya by 10–11 PM. Light dinner at a konbini near the machiya — Fushimi Inari is at 5:30 AM tomorrow, so sleep is the priority.

Accommodation · Tonight

Kyoto machiya (same property as July 10–13) — Kiyu, Natsume-an, or Kohaku-an, Nakagyo-ku. Two more nights here (July 19–21) before the Kumano pilgrimage begins.

Alternative Route · More Ambitious
This version trades Sapporo city time for Daisetsuzan National Park, Hokkaido's volcanic mountain wilderness. Base in Asahikawa instead of Sapporo, cover Biei + Daisetsuzan + Souunkyo Gorge, and end with one strong seafood night in Sapporo. Requires a rental car. More landscape, less city — but a deeper Hokkaido experience.

Friday, July 17 — Tokyo → Asahikawa · Biei

Day 09
Morning · Fly Tokyo → Asahikawa

Fly direct from Haneda to Asahikawa Airport (ANA/JAL, ~1h45m). Rent a 7-seater car at the airport — this is non-negotiable for the mountain itinerary. International Driving Permit required.

Afternoon · Shirogane Blue Pond & Biei Hills

Shirogane Blue Pond (Aoiike) — The vivid turquoise pond with ghostly dead trees rising from the surface. Created by aluminum hydroxide particles in the water — genuinely surreal. 1 hour from Asahikawa Airport.

Biei Patchwork Road — Rolling farmland in different crop colors: golden wheat, green corn, purple lavender. Drive the circuit from Ken and Mary's Tree viewpoint. Late afternoon light is ideal.

Optional · Otokoyama Sake Museum

If timing allows on the way back to Asahikawa. Free tasting of Hokkaido's most celebrated sake. Small but excellent.

Evening · Stay in Asahikawa

Practical base for tonight — nothing fancy needed. Asahikawa is a logistics stop. Jingisukan (Hokkaido lamb BBQ) for dinner at a local spot — a Hokkaido signature that doesn't require a reservation.

Accommodation · Tonight

Business hotel in Asahikawa — any JR-affiliated or Toyoko Inn near Asahikawa Station works. This is purely logistical; save your energy for the mountain day tomorrow.

Saturday, July 18 — Daisetsuzan & Ryokan Night

Day 10
Morning · Asahidake Ropeway & Hiking

Asahidake is Hokkaido's highest peak (2,291m) and the core of Daisetsuzan National Park — Japan's largest national park. Take the Asahidake Ropeway to the 1,600m station. In July: alpine wildflowers, volcanic fumaroles, and panoramic ridge views. This is your core nature day.

Light hiking options from the ropeway top station: the Sugatami Pond Circuit (1.7km, ~1 hour, family-friendly) winds past steaming vents, mirror-still ponds, and snowfields that persist into July. For more challenge: the trail toward the summit (3-4 hrs round trip, intermediate).

Afternoon · Slow Down Intentionally

This is the day to decompress. After the hike, return to the ropeway base and drive to your ryokan. Arrive in time for the outdoor onsen before dinner. The mountain air, the silence, the hot mineral water — this is the deepest contrast with the rest of your trip.

Evening · Ryokan Check-In at Asahidake Onsen

Check into your ryokan in the Asahidake Onsen village. This is the perfect ryokan location for this itinerary — remote, atmospheric, mountain views from the outdoor bath. Kaiseki dinner and first onsen soak.

Recommended Ryokan · Asahidake Onsen La Vista Daisetsuzan — European-styled mountain lodge with three types of private-use hot spring baths, mountain-view rooms with tatami floors, and excellent kaiseki dinner using Hokkaido ingredients. Family rooms available (children welcome). 7-minute walk to Asahidake Visitor Center, 700m from the ropeway. Breakfast buffet features Hokkaido scallops, salmon roe, and Toro salmon. ~¥25,000–45,000/person with dinner + breakfast. Book at selected-ryokan.com.

Alternative: Asahidake Onsen Hotel Bear Monte — Modern, spacious rooms, multiple temperature-varied onsen pools + sauna. 3-minute walk to ropeway. English-friendly, international guest reviews consistently strong (9.2 comfort on Booking.com). Dinner + breakfast included. More affordable: ~¥15,000–30,000/person. Book at bearmonte.jp.

Budget-friendly: Yumoto Yukomansou — Four hot spring baths + three private baths, all naturally flowing. Two dinner options: French course with Hokkaido ingredients, or hot-pot/teppanyaki. Oldest lodge in Asahidake Onsen area. ~¥12,000–25,000/person.
Ryokan & Onsen — Historical ContextThe ryokan tradition dates to the 8th century when imperial messengers required lodging along official routes. The format — tatami rooms, futon, yukata robes, kaiseki dinner, communal or private onsen baths — has remained essentially unchanged for over 1,000 years. Hot spring (onsen) culture is intertwined with both Shinto purification ritual and Buddhist ascetic practice. The mineral content of each spring is classified and named; different springs are said to treat different ailments — a tradition documented in texts from the Nara period (8th century).

Sunday, July 19 — Souunkyo Gorge → Sapporo

Day 11
Morning · Ryokan Breakfast & Onsen

Wake at 5:30–6 AM for the outdoor rotenburo before other guests are up. Morning kaiseki breakfast: miso soup, grilled fish, rice, pickles, tamagoyaki, tofu. Allow 2+ hours before checkout.

Late Morning · Souunkyo Gorge (層雲峡)

Drive northeast to Souunkyo Gorge (~1.5 hrs from Asahidake) — a dramatic 24km canyon carved through columnar basalt cliffs. Short walks to Ryusei Falls (Meteor Falls) and Ginga Falls (Milky Way Falls), a pair of twin waterfalls dropping from the cliff face. Optional: Souunkyo Ropeway + Chairlift to Kurodake 7th Station for more mountain views (¥2,400 round trip, adds ~1.5 hrs).

Afternoon · Drive to Sapporo

Drive south to Sapporo (~2.5–3 hours via Expressway). Return rental car at Sapporo Station or at a drop-off near your hotel. This is your urban reset after two days in the mountains.

Evening · Sapporo Seafood Reward Night

This is your one Sapporo night — make it count. Nijo Market for fresh uni and crab if you didn't hit it on arrival. Then a proper seafood dinner: Kani Honke for crab kaiseki (¥8,000–20,000/person, reserve ahead), or the Susukino area for a spread of Hokkaido sashimi and Sapporo draft beer.

Accommodation · Tonight

Sapporo central hotel — Odori or Susukino area. One night only. Tomorrow: fly CTS → KIX (Osaka Kansai, ~1h45m) or CTS → ITM (Osaka Itami), then Haruka Express or Limousine Bus to Kyoto. Arrive Kyoto machiya by late afternoon/evening — Fushimi Inari at 5:30 AM on July 20, so rest is essential.

Critical Constraints for Plan B
• Rental car is non-negotiable — public transit cannot cover this route in 3 days
• Fly into Asahikawa (not Sapporo) — saves 2+ hours of driving on Day 1
• You are NOT deeply exploring Sapporo — just one strong evening. Accept this trade-off
• Do NOT try to add Otaru — wrong direction, too much driving
• Keep Daisetsuzan hikes light — the Sugatami Pond Circuit is enough with kids
• International Driving Permit required — get from AAA before departure (1-2 weeks processing)
Itinerary Map

Hokkaido — Key Sites

Sapporo, the Furano/Biei farming region, and the Niseko/Jozankei ryokan area.

Where to Eat

Hokkaido — Restaurants

Hokkaido is Japan's primary seafood and dairy region. The cuisine is distinct from the rest of Japan — richer, more robust, centered on crab, fresh seafood, lamb, and exceptional dairy.

High End
Kani Honke
Sapporo Center · Crab Kaiseki · ¥8,000–20,000

The definitive Hokkaido crab restaurant. Whole hairy crab, king crab, snow crab — prepared every possible way across a multi-course kaiseki. Hokkaido crab in July is at summer peak. Once-in-a-trip meal.

Classic
Daruma Jingisukan
Susukino · BBQ Lamb · ¥2,000–3,500

Jingisukan — Hokkaido's signature dish. Lamb and vegetables grilled on a dome-shaped iron grill at your table. The Sapporo Susukino branch is the historic original. Loud, smoky, essential.

Ryokan Kaiseki
Zaborin / Jozankei Tsuruga
Hokkaido Mountains · Kaiseki · Included in stay

The evening kaiseki at the ryokan is purpose-built for the location — Hokkaido seasonal ingredients, mountain vegetables, and fresh seafood prepared with the same philosophy as Kyoto cuisine but in a northern register.

Miso Ramen
Suage+ at Ramen Republic
Sapporo Station (Esta B1) · Miso Ramen · ¥1,000–1,500

Sapporo miso ramen — thick, rich, savory broth with butter and corn — is the definitive Hokkaido bowl. Ramen Republic on the 10th floor of Esta mall puts 8 of Sapporo's best under one roof. Suage+ is the strongest pick.

Coffee
Morihiko Coffee
Multiple Sapporo locations · Specialty Coffee

Sapporo's most celebrated coffee culture — small collective of refined café spaces. Exceptional pour-overs and beautiful interiors. Visit for afternoon coffee before the flight home tomorrow.

京都
July 20–21 · Two Days

Kyoto II

Back to Kyoto after Hokkaido — two more days for Fushimi Inari at dawn, Nanzen-ji, and Sanjusangendo before the Kumano pilgrimage begins.

Day-by-Day
Map
Restaurants

Monday, July 20 — Fushimi Inari & Nanzen-ji

Day 12
Dawn · Fushimi Inari (Arrive 5:30 AM)

Fushimi Inari Taisha — thousands of vermilion torii gates winding up the sacred Mt. Inari. The total circuit is 4km; most visitors do only the lower section, but the upper mountain (2+ hours) is far more rewarding, quieter, and atmospherically charged. Arrive at dawn for mist through the gates. Open 24 hours.

Inari Worship — Foxes, Rice & CommerceFounded in 711 AD, Fushimi Inari is the head shrine of over 40,000 Inari shrines across Japan. Inari is the kami of rice, agriculture, foxes, and worldly success — one of the most complex deities in the Shinto pantheon. The fox (kitsune) is Inari's messenger, not Inari itself. The torii gates are donated by businesses and individuals as expressions of gratitude; each bears the donor's name and date. See Encyclopedia of Shinto on Inari worship and World History Encyclopedia on Inari for scholarly context.
Afternoon · Nanzen-ji

Nanzen-ji is one of the most important Rinzai Zen temples in Japan — head of the Nanzen-ji branch of the school. See The Record of Rinzai for the foundational text of this tradition. The incongruous 19th-century Roman brick aqueduct running through the temple grounds represents the Meiji government's use of Buddhist temple land for industrial infrastructure — a fascinating collision of eras. The Tenjuan garden with carp pond is exceptional in summer.

Accommodation · July 20–21

Kyoto machiya (same property as first visit, July 10–13) — Kiyu, Natsume-an, or Kohaku-an, Nakagyo-ku. Two final nights before the Kumano Kodo pilgrimage begins.

🏨 Gupta Family — Hilton Garden Inn Kyoto Shijo Karasuma · July 20–22
397 Gojokarasumacho, Shimogyo Ward, Kyoto · Tel: +81 75-320-5111 · Conf. 3447591220
Check-in July 20, check-out July 22 — same two nights as us. The hotel is ~1.5 km west of the machiya in the Shimogyo district, walkable in 20 minutes.

Tuesday, July 21 — Sanjusangendo & Preparation

Day 13
Morning · Sanjusangendo

Sanjusangendo (Temple of 33 Bays) houses 1,001 life-size gilded wooden statues of Kannon Bosatsu (the thousand-armed Avalokiteśvara), each subtly different, arranged in the world's longest wooden building (120 meters). Built 1164, rebuilt 1266. The central large Kannon was sculpted by Tankei, one of the great masters of the Kamakura period, who was still carving at age 80. No photography inside. ¥600.

Kannon Iconography & Kamakura SculptureThe thousand-armed Kannon represents the bodhisattva's infinite capacity to aid all suffering beings — each hand holding a different implement of liberation. The Kamakura period (1185–1333) produced Japan's most psychologically powerful sculpture: more realistic, more emotionally direct than Heian-period work. For the theological background see The Zen Site and the Metropolitan Museum's essay on Kamakura Buddhist sculpture.
Afternoon · Logistics for Kumano

Pack light daypacks for the Kumano section — you'll be carrying these for three days on the trail. Konbini run before bed: onigiri, nuts, energy bars, electrolyte drinks. Review walking route and minshuku confirmations. Optional morning/afternoon: Tofuku-ji for its Shigemori Mirei garden (1939) — one of the most important modern garden works in Japan, blending Zen tradition with modernist geometry.

📦 Tonight — Ship Bags to Hiroshima (Yamato Transport) · Critical
This is the most important Yamato drop of the trip. You walk the Kumano Kodo for three days with daypacks only — heavy bags must go ahead now. Drop at a 7-Eleven near the Kyoto machiya tonight — look for the black cat (クロネコ) logo. Address to your Hiroshima hotel (Sheraton Grand Hiroshima or Hotel Granvia Hiroshima — write address in Japanese). Drop off before 6–7 PM; allow 1–2 days delivery so bags should arrive by July 26.
  • The machiya host can print or write the Japanese address for you — ask ahead of time
  • Fill out the delivery slip at the counter; payment in cash
  • Keep only: daypack, trail snacks, one change of clothes, medications, camera, rain gear
  • The minshuku lodges provide yukata robes and toiletries — you need less than you think
Accommodation · Tonight

Kyoto machiya — last night (July 21). Early departure tomorrow: JR to Osaka, then Kinokuni Line toward Kii-Tanabe. Aim to be at Kyoto Station by 7–8 AM.

✈️ Asha & Sahana (Gupta Daughters) — Flying Home Tomorrow · July 22
Asha and Sahana check out of the Hilton Garden Inn July 22 and fly home from Narita (NRT) at 17:25. To make that flight they'll likely travel to Tokyo tonight (July 21) — the Kyoto → Tokyo shinkansen + Narita Express is ~4 hours minimum. They arrive back in New York (LaGuardia) the evening of July 22 via Toronto.
  • AC10 — Tokyo Narita (NRT) 17:25 → Toronto (YYZ) 16:30 · Air Canada Boeing 787-9 · 12h 05m
  • AC720 — Toronto (YYZ) 19:25 → New York LaGuardia (LGA) 20:59
Tonight may be the last chance to say goodbye before the Kumano trail swallows us for three days.
🔀
Alternative for this day
Skip Sanjusangendo (or do it at 8 AM in 90 min) and spend the day in Nara + Osaka as day trips from Kyoto. You still stay in Kyoto tonight and pack for Kumano in the evening.

Tuesday, July 21 — Alternative: Nara & Osaka Day Trip

Day 13 · Alt Option
Logistics Accommodation unchanged — you sleep in Kyoto tonight and tomorrow. If you want Sanjusangendo, do it at 8 AM (opens at 8, takes 90 min, right near Kyoto Station). Nara is 45 min from Kyoto on the Kintetsu Kyoto Line. Osaka is 40 min from Nara on Kintetsu, or 30 min from Kyoto directly. Back in Kyoto by 7:30–8 PM to pack for Kumano.
8:00 AM (Optional) · Sanjusangendo — Quick Morning Visit

Open at 8 AM, only 90 minutes needed for the full hall. 1,001 Kannon statues, Tankei's great central figure, and a quiet interior before the tour groups arrive. Walk to Kyoto Station (15 min) and board the Kintetsu Kyoto Line for Nara.

10:00 AM · Nara — Tōdai-ji & Kasuga Taisha

Nara (奈良) was Japan's first permanent capital (710–794 AD), preceding Kyoto by 84 years. The religious landscape it produced is in direct continuity with everything you're seeing in Kyoto — this is not a detour but a deepening.

Tōdai-ji (東大寺, "Great Eastern Temple") was founded by Emperor Shōmu in 752 as the head of a national network of provincial temples — the first systematic deployment of Buddhism as state infrastructure. The Daibutsuden (Great Buddha Hall) is the largest wooden building in the world. Inside: the Roshana Butsu — a 15-meter bronze Vairoćana Buddha, one of the most ambitious religious objects ever created in Japan. The casting consumed the country's bronze supply and took decades. The 752 dedication ceremony drew 10,000 monks from across Asia.

Tōdai-ji and the Nara Buddhist State Emperor Shōmu commissioned the Great Buddha during a decade of crisis: repeated smallpox epidemics killed up to a third of the population in the 730s, famines and aristocratic rebellions followed. The casting was a state-sponsored act of cosmic petition — Buddhism deployed simultaneously as administrative ideology and eschatological insurance. The provincial temple network (kokubunji) radiating from Tōdai-ji was Japan's first experiment in ideological governance through religious institutions. See World History Encyclopedia on Tōdai-ji and Sacred Texts Buddhist Archive for primary source context.

Kasuga Taisha (春日大社) — 15 min walk from Tōdai-ji through the deer park. Founded 768 AD as the tutelary shrine of the Fujiwara clan. Over 3,000 stone and bronze lanterns line the forest approaches. The sacred deer (shika) of Nara Park are messengers of the shrine's deities — semi-wild, accustomed to humans, and will bow for shika senbei (deer crackers, ¥200 from stalls everywhere). See Encyclopedia of Shinto on Kasuga Taisha.

Tōdai-ji · ¥800 · Great Buddha Hall Kasuga Taisha · Free outer precinct Shika Senbei · ¥200 · Feed the deer
1:30 PM · Nara Lunch, Then Kintetsu to Osaka

Kakinoha-zushi (柿の葉寿司) — pressed sushi wrapped in persimmon leaves — is Nara's signature food, available at shops near Kintetsu Nara Station. Light and excellent. Then board the Kintetsu Nara Line direct to Osaka Namba (45 min, ¥680) — no need to backtrack through Kyoto.

2:30 PM · Osaka Castle (大阪城)

Osaka Castle was originally built by Toyotomi Hideyoshi beginning in 1583 — conceived as the most powerful castle in Japan and the seat of his national hegemony after decades of civil war. It was besieged and destroyed in 1615 when Tokugawa forces eliminated the last Toyotomi heir, ending the Sengoku period and beginning the Edo era. The current tower is a 1931 ferro-concrete reconstruction housing a well-designed museum of Toyotomi-era history. The original stone walls (some 16th-century construction) and the moat system are historically authentic and impressive at scale. Allow 90 minutes for the castle and grounds.

Osaka Castle and the Toyotomi Moment Hideyoshi's original castle was a deliberate performance of power: taller and more opulent than anything built before in Japan. The 1615 Siege of Osaka is one of the most consequential events in Japanese history — it eliminated the last serious rival to Tokugawa authority and secured the conditions for 250 years of Edo-era stability. The museum inside the reconstruction covers this history well. See World History Encyclopedia on Osaka Castle.

After the castle, 20 min by subway to Dotonbori — Osaka's neon entertainment canal district. Keep it to 30–40 minutes: a walk, takoyaki from a street stall, the Glico Man billboard for the kids. Then Osaka back to Kyoto (30 min on the shinkansen or 45 min on the JR rapid line).

Osaka Castle · ¥600 · Museum + original stone walls Takoyaki in Dotonbori · ¥600 · Osaka essential Back in Kyoto by 7:30 PM · Pack for Kumano
Evening · Return to Kyoto — Pack for Kumano

Osaka → Kyoto by 8 PM. Light dinner near the machiya — this is not a night for a major restaurant. Pack daypacks for the Kumano Kodo section. Send heavy luggage via Yamato Transport to your Hiroshima or Tokyo hotel. Confirm minshuku reservations. Early sleep: the pilgrimage begins tomorrow.

Itinerary Map

Kyoto II — Key Sites

The southeastern and northern Kyoto sites for our return visit.

Where to Eat

Kyoto II — Restaurants

These two days are best served by lighter meals — save appetite for the pilgrimage section ahead and the Ise/Hiroshima stops to come.

For the Dawn Return
Hinode Udon
Fushimi · Udon · ¥600–1,000

Opens at 5 AM specifically for Fushimi Inari visitors. Simple hot udon in dashi broth — the right return meal after a dawn mountain walk. Cash only. A few hundred meters from the main shrine.

Bib Gourmand
Kagizen Yoshifusa
Gion · Historic Sweets · Est. 1715

Over 300 years old. Famous for kuzukiri (arrowroot noodles in sweet kuromitsu syrup) and seasonal wagashi. The interior is a time capsule. Best sweets experience in Gion for an afternoon break.

Final Kyoto Dinner
En
Gion · Kyoto Cuisine · ¥4,000–8,000

Modern Kyoto obanzai cuisine in a renovated machiya. Seasonal small plates, excellent sake selection, beautiful space. The right farewell dinner before the pilgrimage begins.

熊野
July 22–24 · Three Days

Kumano Kodo

One of two UNESCO-listed pilgrimage routes in the world (the other is the Camino de Santiago, with which it has a formal twinning). Three days walking through ancient cedar forest, sacred shrines, and mountain onsen.

Day-by-Day
Map
Restaurants

Wednesday, July 22 — Into Kumano

Day 14
Morning · Travel to Takijiri (Full Transit Day)

From Kyoto Station: take the JR Osaka Loop Line south to Osaka (~15 min), then board the JR Kinokuni Line limited express (Kuroshio) to Kii-Tanabe (~2.5 hrs, ~¥5,500/person). From Kii-Tanabe Station: take the Meiko Bus to Takijiri-Oji (~60 min, ~¥1,000) — the traditional gateway to the Nakahechi route. Total transit: ~3.5–4 hours from Kyoto. Reserve minshuku accommodation in advance via Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau (fill up months ahead in July).

Afternoon · First Kumano Walk (Takijiri → Chikatsuyu)

From Takijiri-Oji — a small wooden shrine at the forest edge — the route immediately enters primary cedar forest. The stone-paved paths here are original 12th-century construction. The oji (subsidiary shrines) appear at regular intervals; each is a rest point and a reminder that this path was designed for spiritual travel, not tourism.

Accommodation · Tonight (Day 14)

Minshuku on the Nakahechi route — book via tb-kumano.jp/en/stay. Three different minshuku lodges across the three nights (July 22–24), each arranged by the Tanabe City Kumano Tourism Bureau. Dinner and breakfast included — home-cooked mountain food after a day of walking.

✈️ Asha & Sahana (Gupta) — Flying Home Today from Narita
While we begin the pilgrimage trail, Asha and Sahana are in the air heading home. Their flight departs Narita (NRT) at 17:25 — they'll be somewhere over the Pacific as we settle into our first minshuku.
AC10 — NRT 17:25 → Toronto YYZ 16:30 · then AC720 — YYZ 19:25 → New York LaGuardia 20:59
Kumano and Syncretic ReligionThe Kumano Sanzan has been the center of Japanese pilgrimage for over 1,000 years. What makes it philosophically unusual is its radical inclusivity: it was open to women, commoners, and members of both Buddhist and Shinto traditions at a time when most sacred sites were exclusionary. The blending of Shinto mountain worship (Shugendo), Buddhism, and Taoist elements at Kumano represents the fullest expression of shinbutsu shūgō (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) in Japanese religious history. See UNESCO World Heritage listing for the Sacred Sites and Pilgrimage Routes of the Kii Mountain Range, Tanabe City's history of the Kumano Kodo, and the scholarly treatment in World History Encyclopedia on Shugendo. For the Buddhist doctrinal background, see Stanford Encyclopedia on Japanese religious thought and The Zen Site.

Thursday, July 23 — Nachi Taisha & Seiganto-ji

Day 15
Afternoon · Kumano Nachi Taisha & Seiganto-ji

Kumano Nachi Taisha is one of the three grand shrines of the Kumano complex, set on the mountainside above Japan's highest single-drop waterfall (Nachi, 133m). The three-story pagoda viewed against the falls is one of the most recognized images in Japanese religious art.

Seiganto-ji is the Buddhist temple directly adjacent to Nachi Taisha, sharing the same sacred ground. This coexistence embodies shinbutsu shūgō — the syncretic tradition where Buddhist and Shinto practice occupied the same sacred space for over 1,300 years, until the Meiji government's forced separation in 1868 (shinbutsu bunri). Seiganto-ji is the first temple on the 33-temple Saigoku Kannon pilgrimage route. The violence of the 1868 separation — statues removed, temple iconography destroyed — is still visible in sites like this, where the Buddhist structure was stripped back and the Shinto shrine reasserted. See The Zen Site, Encyclopedia of Shinto on shinbutsu bunri, and Stanford Encyclopedia on Japanese Buddhism.

Seiganto-ji · Intellectually essentialNachi Falls · 133m · Japan's tallest
Evening · Yunomine Onsen

Yunomine Onsen — a hot-spring village operating continuously for 1,800 years. The tsuboyu bath house is UNESCO World Heritage listed as the only onsen to hold this distinction — it accommodates 2 people and must be reserved on the day (¥770). The water changes color three times daily. See Tanabe City accommodation for the best lodging options adjacent to the spring.

Accommodation · Tonight (Day 15)

Minshuku near Yunomine Onsen — the second of three Kumano lodges. Many are directly beside the hot spring village. Dinner included; the tsuboyu UNESCO bath is a short walk.

Friday, July 24 — Kumano Hongu Taisha & Transfer to Ise

Day 16
Morning · Kumano Hongu Taisha

Kumano Hongu Taisha is the principal shrine of the Kumano trio, set on a forested hill. The giant torii near the original flood-destroyed shrine site (Oyunohara clearing) is one of the largest wooden torii in Japan. Spend the morning here without rushing. Visit the treasure hall (¥300). The walk through tall cedar to the main worship hall is one of the most meditative single experiences of the entire trip. See Encyclopedia of Shinto on Kumano worship.

Afternoon · Transfer to Ise (~2.5–3 hrs)

From Kumano Hongu: Meiko Bus to Shingu Station (~1 hr, ~¥1,500). At Shingu, board the JR Kinokuni Line heading northeast along the coast to Taki Station (~1.5 hrs). At Taki, transfer to the Kintetsu Taki Line to Ise-Shi Station (~20 min). Alternatively, some services run directly to Isuzugawa or Ujiyamada. The coastal stretch between Shingu and Taki — Pacific ocean on one side, forested mountains on the other — is one of the most beautiful rail journeys in Japan.

Accommodation · Tonight (Day 16)

Hoshidekan — traditional family inn 5 min from Naiku (Inner Shrine), Ise. Breakfast included (proper Japanese set: grilled fish, miso, rice, pickles). Long-running, family-operated, quiet neighborhood. Perfect base for an early start at Geku tomorrow morning.

Itinerary Map

Kumano Kodo — Key Sites

The Nakahechi route from Takijiri to Hongu, with Nachi Taisha and Yunomine Onsen on the coast.

Where to Eat

Kumano Kodo — Restaurants

The Kumano Kodo is not a foodie destination — the point is simplicity. Minshuku meals are an authentic expression of mountain cuisine and should be embraced, not resisted.

Essential
Minshuku Meals (Included)
Nakahechi Route · Set Dinner & Breakfast

Minshuku set dinners are generous home-cooked meals: mountain vegetables, river fish, tofu, pickles, rice, miso soup — exactly the food needed after a day's walking. The hospitality is genuine and irreplaceable. This is the food experience on this section.

Yunomine
Azumaya Ryokan Restaurant
Yunomine Onsen · Traditional · ¥2,000–4,000

The small restaurant at Azumaya Ryokan serves simple but well-prepared Kumano regional cuisine — river fish, mountain vegetables, local sake. Best option for the Yunomine overnight.

Trail Sustenance
Konbini Onigiri
Any convenience store before Takijiri

Load up on onigiri, nuts, dried fruit, and energybar equivalents before entering the trail. Food options on the Nakahechi are limited to minshuku. This is intentional and appropriate.

伊勢
July 25 · One Day

Ise

The most sacred site in Shinto. After days immersed in Buddhist pilgrimage, Ise's profound Shinto purity offers the most complete possible counterpoint — and the philosophical fulcrum of the whole trip.

Day-by-Day
Map
Restaurants

Saturday, July 25 — Ise Jingu

Day 17
Morning · Geku (Outer Shrine)

The proper visiting order is Geku first. Geku enshrines Toyouke Ōmikami, the deity of food, agriculture, and textiles — a provisioner of the sacred. The unpainted cypress architecture, thatched roofs, and gravel paths establish an aesthetic vocabulary of radical reduction. No gilding, no color, no ornamentation. See official Ise Jingu site and Encyclopedia of Shinto on Ise for the theological background of the shrine complex.

Ise and Shikinen SenguIse Jingu is the holiest site in Shinto and the spiritual home of the imperial family. The main shrine buildings are rebuilt exactly every 20 years in a ritual called Shikinen Sengu — performed continuously since 690 AD. The current structures date to 2013; the next rebuilding is 2033. The ritual keeps ancient construction techniques alive by transferring them from master to apprentice. The inner sanctum has never been photographed and is accessible only to the Emperor and highest priests. This perpetual renewal rather than preservation is philosophically opposed to Western monumentalism — the building is never old. See World History Encyclopedia on Ise and Encyclopedia of Shinto on Amaterasu.
Mid-Morning · Naiku (Inner Shrine)

Naiku enshrines Amaterasu Ōmikami (the Sun Goddess), the divine ancestor of the imperial family and the central deity of the Shinto pantheon. Cross the Uji Bridge (boundary between ordinary and sacred space). The inner precincts are restricted — the experience is of approaching, not entering, which is itself a Shinto teaching about the nature of the sacred. The forested approach on either side of the bridge is extraordinary. See Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy on Shinto for the philosophical implications of Shinto concepts of presence and purity.

Afternoon · Okage Yokocho & Transfer to Hiroshima

The historic shopping street near Naiku, preserved in Edo-period style. Try Ise Udon (thick, soft udon in rich tare — found only in Ise) at Iseya Honten. Buy Akafuku mochi (traditional sweet rice cakes, made fresh and sold same-day) and fresh abalone skewers. Allow 90 minutes before heading to the station.

Transfer to Hiroshima (~3 hrs total): Kintetsu Limited Express from Ujiyamada → Nagoya (~1.5 hrs, ~¥2,200/person). At Nagoya Station, board the Nozomi shinkansen → Hiroshima (~1.5 hrs, ~¥10,410/person). Arrive Hiroshima by early evening. Check in, light dinner near the hotel — the Peace Museum tomorrow morning requires a clear head.

Ise Udon · Only HereAkafuku Mochi · Buy HereKintetsu → Nagoya → Nozomi → Hiroshima · ~3 hrs
